name: Massimo Vignelli
description: The most uncompromising designer of the Modernist era — New York Subway graphics (1972), American Airlines identity, Knoll catalogs. Vignelli used only six typefaces his entire career (Bodoni, Garamond, Helvetica, Futura, Univers, Times Roman) and argued that anything else was self-indulgent. Grid-disciplined, two-color palettes (often just red + black + white), and absolute typographic authority.
rules: Six typefaces are enough for a lifetime — Bodoni, Garamond, Helvetica, Futura, Univers, Times. No others. Period.
Two-color palette per project — red + black + white, or navy + white + cream, or yellow + black. Never a palette of five.
The grid is sacred. Every element aligns. No exceptions, no 'expressive' off-grid moments.
Typography is not decoration — size, weight, and spacing ARE the design.
No drop shadows. No gradients. No rounded corners. No 'decorative' illustrations. The grid, the color, and the type are the whole toolkit.
Density is honest — a dense Vignelli layout IS the New York Subway map; clarity emerges from hierarchy, not whitespace.
One call-to-action per view. Red for the primary. Black for everything else.
Motion is suspicious — Vignelli designed for print. Use opacity fade only. No translate, no scale, no physics.
references: The Vignelli Canon (Massimo Vignelli, 2010) — free PDF online
NY Subway Graphics Standards Manual (Unimark, 1970; reprint 2014)

critic_persona.role: design auditor in the spirit of Massimo Vignelli

scoring_priorities.direction: demand modular type scale and consistent weight contrast
form follows discipline; avoid arbitrary mixing
